skill.name: video-inpainting
skill.description: Guides AI agents through region-based video inpainting with the RunComfy CLI, including model routing, prompt structure, and escalation to mask-driven ComfyUI workflows.
skill.summary: Run video inpainting workflows through RunComfy for object cleanup, region replacement, and frame-aware edits.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences, URLs, model names, and SAM2 segmentation references. The confirmed risks are legitimate but material: the skill authorizes the RunComfy CLI, sends user media or URLs to RunComfy services, and stores an API token locally or in an environment variable.

medium_findings.title: Authorized RunComfy CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill allows Bash commands matching runcomfy and provides runcomfy run examples that process user-provided prompts and video inputs. This is expected for the skill, but it creates command execution and service-invocation risk if arguments are assembled carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration explicitly permits Bash(runcomfy *), and multiple examples invoke runcomfy run. The command scope is limited to the RunComfy CLI, so this is operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Network Processing of User Video Content
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill routes video URLs and prompts to RunComfy model APIs and documents outbound RunComfy endpoints. Users should expect video content, URLs, prompts, and generated outputs to be handled by an external service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions say the CLI POSTs to the Model API and lists RunComfy outbound endpoints. This directly confirms external network processing, but the endpoints match the stated purpose of the skill.

medium_findings.title: API Token Storage and Environment Token Use
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill documents runcomfy login storing an API token in a hidden configuration path and also supports RUNCOMFY_TOKEN in CI. The documented 0600 mode is a mitigation, but marketplace users should treat this as credential handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly describes the token path and environment variable. The text also describes file mode 0600, reducing but not eliminating credential exposure risk.

low_findings.title: Global CLI Installation Guidance
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill suggests installing @runcomfy/cli globally or using npx. This is common package-manager usage and includes a warning against piping remote install scripts into a shell, but it still changes the execution environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The npm and npx commands are present in Markdown examples. The instructions are not hidden or obfuscated and include a safer-install warning, so severity is low.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static False Positives for SAM, Weak Crypto, and Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The Windows SAM database alerts refer to SAM2 segmentation tracking, not credential database access. The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts arise from ordinary prose, model names, or words in Markdown documentation; no evidence of cryptographic use, system probing, or sensitive file reads was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows documentation text and model-routing guidance, not code accessing Windows SAM, weak algorithms, or host reconnaissance commands. No corroborating execution logic exists in the single scanned file.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Backtick Execution Alerts in Markdown
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The Ruby or shell backtick alerts are caused by Markdown inline code and fenced command examples. The actual executable permission boundary is the allowed Bash(runcomfy *) declaration, which is covered as a separate medium finding.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited backticks are Markdown formatting and command examples in SKILL.md. They do not represent Ruby backtick execution inside runnable source code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Scope for External CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access for runcomfy commands. This is necessary for the workflow but should remain limited to the documented command prefix and should not be expanded without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools metadata and security section both define the RunComfy Bash scope. The restriction is clear, but it is still external command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Model API Calls and Downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI posts jobs to RunComfy model APIs, polls status, and downloads generated results into an output directory. This combines network processing with local file writes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly states the CLI posts to the Model API and downloads results. The output directory examples provide evidence of local writes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Credential File
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill states that runcomfy login writes an API token to a hidden configuration file. The documented file permissions reduce risk, but the token is still sensitive local state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential path and permission mode are directly documented on the cited line. This is confirmed credential storage, not inferred behavior.

content.value_statement: Video inpainting across frames is difficult because edits must track motion and preserve surrounding content. This skill helps agents choose the right RunComfy route, build clear prompts, and know when to use mask-driven workflows.
actual_capabilities: Selects Wan 2-7 Edit-Video for prompt-driven region edits across video frames.
Routes identity-stable restyle requests to Lucy Edit Restyle when appropriate.
Uses Seedream 4-0 Edit-Sequential guidance for short frame-stack editing workflows.
Shows how to structure RunComfy CLI inputs, output folders, and preservation prompts.
Explains when pixel-precise mask propagation requires RunComfy ComfyUI workflows.
Documents token handling, outbound endpoints, and shell-injection boundaries.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy account, and valid authentication.
Prompt-driven endpoints do not provide pixel-perfect masks or guaranteed temporal consistency.
User media, URLs, and prompts may be processed by RunComfy external services.
Long clips or precise replacements may need manual review and ComfyUI workflows.

use_cases.title: Clean Licensed Production Footage
use_cases.description: Remove a distracting object, cable, or approved overlay from a clip while preserving the rest of the scene.

use_cases.title: Restyle a Tracked Region
use_cases.description: Change an outfit, prop, or localized visual style across frames when identity and motion should remain stable.

use_cases.title: Choose an Inpainting Workflow
use_cases.description: Decide whether a CLI model endpoint is enough or whether a mask-driven ComfyUI workflow is required.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Object Cleanup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for removing one visible object.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use video-inpainting to remove the object I identify in this clip. Preserve all other content and explain the RunComfy route you choose.

prompt_templates.title: Spatial Region Edit
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request with a described region and preservation constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit the described region across all frames using spatial language. Keep lighting, motion, camera movement, and unrelated areas unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Identity-Stable Restyle
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced creative change that must track a person or object.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the best RunComfy video inpainting route for an identity-stable restyle. Explain why Lucy Edit Restyle or Wan 2-7 is the better option.

prompt_templates.title: Mask-Precision Workflow Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request requiring surgical region control.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a pixel-precise video inpainting workflow. Tell me when the CLI is insufficient and which RunComfy ComfyUI workflow class to use.

output_examples.input: Remove a small object in the lower-right corner of a short product video.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Wan 2-7 Edit-Video, describes the region in plain language, adds preservation instructions, and saves results to an output folder.

output_examples.input: Replace a prop across several moving frames while keeping the actor consistent.
output_examples.output: The skill compares Wan 2-7 and Lucy Edit Restyle, then recommends the route that better preserves identity and motion.

output_examples.input: A precise mask must follow a moving subject through the clip.
output_examples.output: The skill explains that a prompt-only CLI endpoint is not enough and points to mask-driven RunComfy ComfyUI workflows.

best_practices: Use one edit per run and describe the target region with clear spatial language.
State preservation requirements explicitly, including unchanged areas, lighting, and camera motion.
Use only user-approved source URLs and review outputs before publishing edited media.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to remove ownership marks or rights information without authorization.
Do not combine unrelated edits in one prompt when temporal consistency matters.
Do not send private or regulated video content to external services without approval.

faq.question: Does this skill edit videos directly?
faq.answer: It guides an agent to use the RunComfy CLI and RunComfy model endpoints for region-based video edits.

faq.question: Which model is the default route?
faq.answer: Wan 2-7 Edit-Video is the default route for prompt-driven region edits across frames.

faq.question: Can it use exact masks?
faq.answer: The CLI route is prompt-driven. Exact mask propagation usually requires a RunComfy ComfyUI workflow.

faq.question: Does it require authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. The RunComfy CLI needs login or a RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable.

faq.question: Where are outputs saved?
faq.answer: The examples save generated files to a local output directory selected in the RunComfy CLI command.

faq.question: Is it safe for private footage?
faq.answer: Use caution. The workflow can send video URLs, prompts, and generated content to RunComfy services.
